perturbation.original_text:
Either party may terminate this Agreement without penalty and without prejudice to any claims arising prior to termination as follows:
(a) Upon the written agreement of both parties.
(b) Upon the other party's breach of this Agreement provided the terminating party has given written notice of the breach to the other party specifying the breach, the action necessary to cure the breach and the breaching party has not cured the breach within five business days after notice is given of any failure by NPC to provide any funds required hereunder to be provided by NPC to pay Merchants or within thirty business days after notice is given of any other breach.
perturbation.changed_text:
Either party may terminate this Agreement without penalty and without prejudice to any claims arising prior to termination as follows:
(a) Upon the written agreement of both parties.
(b) Upon the other party's breach of this Agreement provided the terminating party has given written notice of the breach to the other party specifying the breach, the action necessary to cure the breach and the breaching party has not cured the breach within two business days after notice is given of any failure by NPC to provide any funds required hereunder to be provided by NPC to pay Merchants or within fifteen business days after notice is given of any other breach.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure periods for breaches, especially the failure to provide funds, creates an inconsistency with general contract law principles requiring reasonable time for cure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Reasonable Time for Cure
perturbation.law_explanation: Kentucky law requires a 'reasonable time' to cure a breach of contract. Reducing the cure period to two and fifteen business days, respectively, for failure to provide funds or other breaches may not be considered reasonable, potentially conflicting with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the cure period for breaches, potentially creating an inconsistency with legal requirements for 'reasonable time' to cure, as suggested by the Law Explanation. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where time-sensitive obligations do not align with legal requirements. The Perturbation Explanation also highlights this inconsistency. Since there are no law snippets, the contradiction is determined based on the Perturbation Explanation, Law Explanation and definitions.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall automatically renew for successive one-year terms unless one party gives the other party written notice of non-renewal at least six months prior to automatic renewal.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall automatically renew for successive one-year terms unless one party gives the other party written notice of non-renewal at least thirty days prior to automatic renewal.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for non-renewal may conflict with industry standards or implied duties of good faith, especially given the complexity of the relationship.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Reasonable Notice for Termination
perturbation.law_explanation: Kentucky law requires 'reasonable notification' when an agreement is terminated. Reducing the notice period from six months to thirty days for non-renewal may be deemed unreasonable, particularly considering the complex operational and financial ties between the parties.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires six months' notice for non-renewal, while the changed text reduces this to thirty days. The Law Explanation states that Kentucky law requires 'reasonable notification' for agreement termination, and reducing the notice period to thirty days may be deemed unreasonable, especially considering the complex ties between the parties. Since the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition includes scenarios where a contract sets a deadline that potentially violates state law, this change creates a legal contradiction because a thirty-day notice period may not be considered 'reasonable' under Kentucky law.
